## [00:00 - 03:01]

Okay, so in this lesson we're going to take a look at Quadlock and how we've applied the lessons we've just learned to our business. So first lesson was market validation and for us back in 2011 when we thought we'd kick off with Quadlock, it was an idea and we thought the market would like it but we didn't know we did the classic thing of we did a Kickstarter campaign and we showed people this is our idea, we've got an iPhone case and there's going to be a mount and the two can mate together and this is going to be the way you put your iPhone on your bike and we ran that campaign, turned out people liked the idea so we thought yep there's enough people out there that care enough to get involved, we've got something. And then we went on to product validation. So what I'd like to talk about here is when we were already had been doing the iPhone bike mount for a few years and we thought we saw that there was people that were trying to put it on their motorcycle and do something like this so what we actually did to sort of validate that product was the product we already had, we put it on a motorcycle specific page, we just took some photos, put some information there, showed the kind of types of motorcycles, our existing product matched and did well with, didn't cover all motorcycles but we just put it up there, ran some Facebook ads and straight away I think we sold like almost $60,000 and like I think it was maybe four or five weeks of that product we thought, geez okay there's a market fit there for this new product but the product isn't quite right, it does the job for some people not for everyone, if we can really nail this quad lock motorcycle product there'll be a really good strong market there for that product and so we thought yeah go back to the drawing board, make this new product and then launch it to the market with a specific product just for that market and at that time it was obvious to us that this new motorcycle product would be a stand-alone flagship onboarding product because it was going out and getting a whole new range of customers that would bring in a whole new range of sales that weren't already doing business with us in any great volume and then when it came to pricing of the new quad lock motorcycle mount we sort of knew that the product would be similar cost to manufacture a little bit more expensive, we knew we could charge a little bit more on the retail price and we knew that effectively shipping and holding stock and all those costs would be similar to what we're already doing so we just knew it would slot right in and it makes business sense and would make pricing sense and that it would make sense to the customer at the end of the day as well. So now at quad lock we're at a stage where we have multiple onboarding products in multiple verticals of the market and we're making hundreds of sales a day but we want to take the business next level once again so it's not that we needed more product, what we needed to do was better understand the customers that we're already selling to every day. So what we did is a big customer survey where we talked to our customers and we all asked them

## [03:02 - 04:56]

questions about what kind of customer did you come for our cycling solution, you come for our running solution, our drive solution and once we understood what solution they were there for we asked them what features about the product was it that first draw you to it, what made you buy that product and then once we started to understand that we could change all our copy or our marketing to and include exactly what the customer wants to hear because at its scale people are all sort of the same and once we knew that we could then go to the market with all those new points that we may have either been prioritizing things that weren't important and deproverizing things that are important and by doing that we just converted better on our ads on our website and just got more customers cheaper. So now's probably a great time to mention that of utmost importance I believe in business is to always be learning and always be looking for opportunity, always be trying to do slightly better than you are doing and when you think you're doing as well as you can you're not, you can always do better, you can always find that extra new thing, that extra just slight optimization whatever it is that you're doing there's always a chance to do multiple parts of it better and when you can at a point all the big wins seem to be gone but you can then find 10 little 1-2 percenters and it gives you you know 10-20 percent growth so just stay open to those ideas, look, test, measure, learn and just do whatever you can to just always be getting better. So as an action item to this I believe probably one of the best things you could do is sort of clean mine and just be honest with yourself and look at what you're doing and come up with three things that you can turn around tomorrow and start making them better come up with three separate things and say if we can just make them 1-2-5 percent better you'll be doing a really great job and once you do that look for another three. Okay guys so I hope you really got some value out of this and found some things that you can apply to your business so I'll see you in the Facebook group.
